content.text: Training data is the second axis of the scaling laws alongside model parameters. The Kaplan et al. (2020) laws showed that loss decreases as a power law in token count, and that for a fixed compute budget, the optimal strategy is to scale data and parameters together. The Hoffmann et al. (2022) "Chinchilla" paper refined this: a model trained on roughly 20 tokens per parameter reaches the compute-optimal frontier. Under-training large models on too little data is wasteful — a 70B model trained on 1T tokens is suboptimal; at 1.4T tokens (20× parameters) it is compute-optimal.
The Chinchilla paper fit a loss function over (N, D) — parameter count and token count — to determine the compute-optimal allocation for a given FLOPs budget

attrs.latex: N_{\text{opt}} \propto C^{0.5}, \quad D_{\text{opt}} \propto C^{0.5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{\text{opt}} \approx 20 \cdot N_{\text{opt}}

content.text: This predicts that compute should be split roughly equally between parameters and data. Earlier models like GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens ≈ 1.7 tokens/param) were significantly under-trained by this criterion. Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens = 20 tokens/param) achieved better benchmark performance despite being 2.5× smaller than GPT-3.

def chinchilla_optimal(
    compute_flops: float | None = None,
    n_params: float | None = None,
    tokens: float | None = None,
    flops_per_token_per_param: float = 6.0,
) -> dict:
    """
    Given any two of (compute, parameters, tokens), compute the third
    and the optimal split according to Chinchilla scaling.

    flops_per_token_per_param: ~6 for a standard transformer forward+backward pass.
    """
    if compute_flops is not None and n_params is not None:
        tokens = compute_flops / (flops_per_token_per_param * n_params)
        return {"tokens": tokens, "tokens_per_param": tokens / n_params,
                "compute_flops": compute_flops, "n_params": n_params}
    elif compute_flops is not None and tokens is not None:
        n_params = compute_flops / (flops_per_token_per_param * tokens)
        return {"n_params": n_params, "tokens_per_param": tokens / n_params,
                "compute_flops": compute_flops, "tokens": tokens}
    elif n_params is not None and tokens is not None:
        compute_flops = flops_per_token_per_param * n_params * tokens
        return {"compute_flops": compute_flops, "tokens_per_param": tokens / n_params,
                "n_params": n_params, "tokens": tokens}
    else:
        raise ValueError("Provide exactly two of: compute_flops, n_params, tokens")


# Optimal data given a fixed model size
for n_b in [1, 7, 13, 70, 405]:   # billions of parameters
    n = n_b * 1e9
    optimal_tokens = 20 * n        # Chinchilla: 20 tokens per parameter
    result = chinchilla_optimal(n_params=n, tokens=optimal_tokens)
    print(f"{n_b:>4}B params → {optimal_tokens/1e9:.0f}B tokens  "
          f"(compute = {result['compute_flops']:.2e} FLOPs)")

#    1B params →  20B tokens  (compute = 1.20e+11 FLOPs)
#    7B params → 140B tokens  (compute = 8.40e+12 FLOPs)
#   13B params → 260B tokens  (compute = 1.56e+13 FLOPs)
#   70B params → 1400B tokens (compute = 8.40e+14 FLOPs)
#  405B params → 8100B tokens (compute = 4.86e+15 FLOPs)

# Real models vs Chinchilla-optimal
print("\nReal models vs Chinchilla-optimal:")
models = [
    # name,          n_params_B, tokens_B
    ("GPT-3",        175,   300),
    ("Chinchilla",    70,  1400),
    ("LLaMA 1 65B",  65,  1400),
    ("LLaMA 3 8B",    8, 15000),   # ~1875 tokens/param — deliberately over-trained
    ("LLaMA 3 70B",  70, 15000),   # ~214 tokens/param
]
for name, n_b, d_b in models:
    ratio = d_b / n_b
    status = "over-trained" if ratio > 30 else ("under-trained" if ratio < 15 else "near-optimal")
    print(f"  {name:<18} {n_b:>4}B params, {d_b:>5}B tokens, {ratio:.0f} tok/param — {status}")

Raw token count is not the only metric. The composition and quality of the training corpus matter significantly. Models like LLaMA 3 deliberately exceed the Chinchilla-optimal token count (15T tokens for a 70B model = 214 tokens/param) because they prioritise the resulting
: an over-trained smaller model beats a Chinchilla-optimal larger model at the same inference cost. This is the "inference-compute-optimal" regime.
Removing near-duplicates reduces memorisation and improves generalisation
MinHash LSH or exact n-gram dedup
More code → better coding; more books → better reasoning
Domain weights tuned on downstream evals
Removing low-quality web pages improves all benchmarks
Perplexity filter, n-gram overlap with reference corpus
More tokens in a language → better generation in that language
Repeating data beyond 1 epoch degrades generalization; <4× repetition usually acceptable
Avoid with large enough unique datasets

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")   # GPT-4 / LLaMA tokenizer (approximate)

def estimate_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

# Typical token densities for common sources
SOURCES = {
    "Web page (average)": 500,        # tokens
    "News article": 800,
    "Book (average, 80K words)": 110_000,
    "ArXiv paper": 8_000,
    "GitHub file (Python, average)": 400,
    "Wikipedia article": 1_500,
}

print("Token budget for a 7B Chinchilla-optimal run: 140B tokens")
print("That's roughly:")
for source, toks in SOURCES.items():
    count = 140_000_000_000 / toks
    print(f"  {count:>15,.0f}  {source}")

# Measure actual density on your data
sample_text = """The transformer architecture revolutionised natural language processing.
    Self-attention allows every token to attend to every other token in the sequence."""
print(f"\nSample text: {estimate_tokens(sample_text)} tokens for {len(sample_text.split())} words")
print(f"Words-per-token ratio: {len(sample_text.split()) / estimate_tokens(sample_text):.2f}")
# Typical English: ~0.75 words per token (1.33 tokens per word)

The Chinchilla result shifted the field from "bigger model = better" to "right-sized model on right-sized data". But the story keeps evolving: LLaMA 3's decision to train the 8B model on 15T tokens (1875 tokens/param, far beyond Chinchilla-optimal) showed that over-training a smaller model is often the right choice when serving cost matters more than training cost. The practical takeaway is that data quantity and quality are both first-class levers — not afterthoughts to be filled with whatever web crawl is available.

Before the Chinchilla paper (2022), the field assumed that bigger models were always better: GPT-3 at 175B parameters, PaLM at 540B. These models were systematically under-trained on data, wasting enormous compute budgets. Chinchilla demonstrated that the optimal strategy for a fixed compute budget is to scale model size and data together — and that most frontier models were using 5–20× too little data.

The practical consequence was immediate: Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens) outperformed GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens) on almost every benchmark despite being 2.5× smaller. The lesson — that training data quantity is a co-equal scaling axis with model parameters — reshaped how every major lab approaches pretraining.
Data Scaling Architecture: The Data Pipeline
The path from raw internet data to a high-quality training corpus involves several stages. Each stage has been studied empirically and has measurable effects on final model quality.

attrs.alt: Scaling laws chart showing loss curves for different model sizes as a function of training tokens, with compute-optimal frontier and model comparisons

content.text: Benefits of Understanding Data Scaling Laws
Knowing the Chinchilla relationship lets you calculate the compute-optimal token count for any model size before beginning a training run, eliminating the costly mistake of under-training.

By deliberately over-training smaller models (LLaMA 3 approach), practitioners achieve better performance at lower serving costs. The one-time training cost is amortised over millions of inference calls.

Scaling laws let you quantify the return on investment for data quality improvements. If increasing dataset quality from 1T to 1.5T effective tokens (via deduplication and filtering) has the same effect as doubling parameters, the data investment is usually far cheaper.

Power law loss curves allow extrapolation: running a small training experiment and fitting the loss curve predicts final performance at full scale, enabling go/no-go decisions before committing full compute.
Best loss for training compute budget
Higher training compute for same quality
Overcomes real-data scarcity for specialised domains
Distribution shift risk; quality control hard
Works when unique data is scarce
When to Use Scaling Law Guidance

Scaling law guidance is most valuable at the planning stage of a pretraining run. Before committing GPU-hours, use the Chinchilla formula to determine the compute-optimal data-to-parameter ratio for your compute budget. If your dataset is smaller than the Chinchilla-optimal token count, either: reduce the model size to match, acquire more data, or accept under-training (sometimes acceptable if you plan extensive fine-tuning afterwards).

Scaling laws are less reliable for fine-tuning (where data quality matters more than quantity), for specialised domains (where domain-specific scaling may differ from the web-data regime studied in Chinchilla), and for instruction tuning (where RLHF alignment data scales differently than pretraining data).
How to Implement: Data Pipeline for Pretraining

A production-grade data pipeline for LLM pretraining involves five stages: collection, deduplication, quality filtering, domain mixing, and tokenisation. Each stage has measurable effects on downstream model quality.

import hashlib
import re
from collections import Counter
from typing import Iterator


# ============================================================
# Stage 1: Deduplication (MinHash-based fuzzy deduplication)
# ============================================================

def compute_minhash(text: str, n_hashes: int = 128, ngram_size: int = 5) -> list[int]:
    """
    Compute MinHash signature for fuzzy deduplication.
    Two documents with Jaccard similarity > 0.8 are considered duplicates.
    This is how FineWeb and Dolma deduplicate web crawls.
    """
    # Extract character n-grams
    ngrams = set()
    for i in range(len(text) - ngram_size + 1):
        ngrams.add(text[i:i+ngram_size])

    # Compute n_hashes minimum hash values (simplified: use different hash functions)
    signature = []
    for seed in range(n_hashes):
        min_val = float("inf")
        for gram in ngrams:
            # In practice, use a proper MinHash library (datasketch)
            h = int(hashlib.md5(f"{seed}:{gram}".encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
            min_val = min(min_val, h)
        signature.append(min_val)
    return signature


def jaccard_from_minhash(sig1: list[int], sig2: list[int]) -> float:
    """Estimate Jaccard similarity from MinHash signatures."""
    return sum(a == b for a, b in zip(sig1, sig2)) / len(sig1)


# ============================================================
# Stage 2: Quality Filtering
# ============================================================

class QualityFilter:
    """
    Heuristic quality filter based on rules used in C4, The Pile, and FineWeb.
    Removes low-quality web pages that would degrade model training.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        min_words: int = 50,
        max_words: int = 100_000,
        min_avg_word_len: float = 3.0,
        max_symbol_ratio: float = 0.2,
        min_alpha_ratio: float = 0.6,
    ):
        self.min_words = min_words
        self.max_words = max_words
        self.min_avg_word_len = min_avg_word_len
        self.max_symbol_ratio = max_symbol_ratio
        self.min_alpha_ratio = min_alpha_ratio

    def is_high_quality(self, text: str) -> tuple[bool, str]:
        words = text.split()
        n_words = len(words)

        if n_words < self.min_words:
            return False, f"too_short ({n_words} words)"
        if n_words > self.max_words:
            return False, f"too_long ({n_words} words)"

        avg_word_len = sum(len(w) for w in words) / n_words
        if avg_word_len < self.min_avg_word_len:
            return False, f"short_words (avg={avg_word_len:.1f})"

        alpha_ratio = sum(1 for c in text if c.isalpha()) / max(len(text), 1)
        if alpha_ratio < self.min_alpha_ratio:
            return False, f"low_alpha ({alpha_ratio:.2f})"

        symbol_ratio = sum(1 for c in text if c in "#@$%^&*[]{}|<>") / max(len(text), 1)
        if symbol_ratio > self.max_symbol_ratio:
            return False, f"high_symbols ({symbol_ratio:.2f})"

        return True, "ok"


# ============================================================
# Stage 3: Domain Mix Estimation
# ============================================================

DOMAIN_WEIGHTS = {
    "web":        0.45,   # Common Crawl / C4
    "books":      0.15,   # Project Gutenberg, BooksCorpus
    "code":       0.15,   # GitHub
    "wikipedia":  0.10,   # Wikipedia dumps
    "arxiv":      0.05,   # ArXiv papers
    "other":      0.10,   # News, forums, etc.
}

def compute_token_budget_per_domain(
    total_tokens: int,
    domain_weights: dict[str, float],
) -> dict[str, int]:
    """
    Calculate how many tokens to sample from each domain given a target total.
    Weights define the proportion of the final training corpus from each source.
    """
    assert abs(sum(domain_weights.values()) - 1.0) < 1e-6, "Weights must sum to 1"
    return {domain: int(total_tokens * w) for domain, w in domain_weights.items()}


# Example: 1T token budget (Chinchilla-optimal for 50B model)
budget = compute_token_budget_per_domain(1_000_000_000_000, DOMAIN_WEIGHTS)
print("Token budget per domain for 1T total:")
for domain, tokens in budget.items():
    print(f"  {domain:<12}: {tokens/1e9:>8.1f}B tokens")


# ============================================================
# Stage 4: Quick pipeline demo
# ============================================================

sample_documents = [
    "This is a high quality article about natural language processing. " * 10,
    "@@@$$$###",                          # fails symbol filter
    "ok yeah lol hi bye",                  # too short
    "This is a high quality article about natural language processing. " * 10,  # duplicate
]

filt = QualityFilter()
seen_sigs = []

print("\nPipeline results:")
for i, doc in enumerate(sample_documents):
    # Quality filter
    keep, reason = filt.is_high_quality(doc)
    if not keep:
        print(f"  Doc {i}: FILTERED ({reason})")
        continue

    # Deduplication (simplified)
    sig = compute_minhash(doc, n_hashes=32)
    is_dup = any(jaccard_from_minhash(sig, seen) > 0.8 for seen in seen_sigs)
    if is_dup:
        print(f"  Doc {i}: DEDUPLICATED")
        continue

    seen_sigs.append(sig)
    print(f"  Doc {i}: KEPT ({len(doc.split())} words)")

Meta's LLaMA 3 (2024) made a deliberate choice to over-train small models beyond Chinchilla-optimal: the 8B model was trained on 15T tokens (1875 tokens per parameter, compared to Chinchilla's 20×=160B). This produced a model that competes with much larger models on reasoning benchmarks while costing far less to serve. The data mix for LLaMA 3 also shifted significantly toward code (4% in LLaMA 1 → much higher in LLaMA 3) and away from pure web text.

# Compute infrastructure required for LLaMA 3's data strategy
# Source: Meta LLaMA 3 technical report (2024)

LLAMA3_CONFIG = {
    "8B": {
        "params": 8e9,
        "tokens": 15e12,          # 15 trillion tokens
        "chinchilla_tokens": 20 * 8e9,  # Chinchilla-optimal: 160B
        "context_length": 8192,
    },
    "70B": {
        "params": 70e9,
        "tokens": 15e12,          # also 15T tokens
        "chinchilla_tokens": 20 * 70e9, # Chinchilla-optimal: 1.4T
        "context_length": 8192,
    },
}

FLOPS_PER_TOKEN_PER_PARAM = 6.0   # forward + backward pass

print("LLaMA 3 data strategy analysis:")
print("-" * 70)
for model, cfg in LLAMA3_CONFIG.items():
    actual_ratio = cfg["tokens"] / cfg["params"]
    optimal_ratio = cfg["chinchilla_tokens"] / cfg["params"]
    over_train_factor = actual_ratio / optimal_ratio
    compute = FLOPS_PER_TOKEN_PER_PARAM * cfg["params"] * cfg["tokens"]

    print(f"LLaMA 3 {model}:")
    print(f"  Parameters:           {cfg['params']/1e9:.0f}B")
    print(f"  Tokens trained:       {cfg['tokens']/1e12:.0f}T")
    print(f"  Chinchilla-optimal:   {cfg['chinchilla_tokens']/1e12:.2f}T tokens")
    print(f"  Over-train factor:    {over_train_factor:.1f}x Chinchilla-optimal")
    print(f"  Training compute:     {compute:.2e} FLOPs")
    print(f"  Approx GPU-hours:     {compute / (312e12 * 3600 * 0.4):.0f} A100 hours")
    print()

# Key insight: 8B model at 15T tokens is 1875/20 = ~94x over-trained
# vs Chinchilla-optimal, but this makes it competitive at inference time
# against Chinchilla-optimal 70B models

# Why over-training pays off:
# - Training compute is a one-time cost
# - Serving cost is paid per request, for the model's lifetime
# - If 1M requests/day, a 3x smaller serving model saves enormous cost
# - LLaMA 3 8B was designed with inference-compute-optimality in mind
print("Inference cost comparison:")
tokens_per_second_per_gpu = {"8B": 2000, "70B": 250}  # approximate
requests_per_day = 1_000_000
tokens_per_request = 500
for model, tps in tokens_per_second_per_gpu.items():
    gpu_seconds = (requests_per_day * tokens_per_request) / tps
    print(f"  {model}: {gpu_seconds/3600:.1f} A100-hours/day to serve 1M requests")

100B tokens of high-quality books and 100B tokens of low-quality forum posts are not equivalent. Domain mix and quality matter as much as raw token count. Over-representing low-quality data can degrade model quality below what a smaller high-quality dataset would achieve.

Without deduplication, large web crawls contain many near-duplicate documents. Training on duplicates causes the model to memorise them and degrades generalisation. Studies show that deduplicated datasets often outperform 2–4× larger non-deduplicated datasets.

Chinchilla scaling laws were derived for pretraining on diverse web data. For instruction tuning or domain fine-tuning, data quality and task alignment matter far more than token count, and the optimal data-to-parameter ratio is very different.

Test sets for common benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K) appear in web crawls. If these are included in training data, benchmark results are inflated. Always check for benchmark contamination before reporting results.
Use the Chinchilla formula as a starting point:
for your target model size. Then adjust based on serving cost requirements — if inference cost is critical, over-train a smaller model.
Use exact hash dedup (SHA-256 on paragraphs) followed by fuzzy dedup (MinHash with 0.8 Jaccard threshold). This typically removes 20–40% of raw web crawl data and significantly improves quality.
Run scaling experiments before full training:

Train 3–5 small models (1B params) at varying token counts (10B, 50B, 100B, 200B tokens) and fit a power law to the loss curve. This predicts final performance and validates your data pipeline quality before committing to a full training run.
Monitor perplexity on held-out evaluation sets:
Use domain-specific held-out data (books, code, scientific papers, news) to track training progress separately per domain. This reveals if the domain mix is degrading any specific capability.
Performance and Scalability of Data Pipelines

Processing 15T tokens for LLaMA 3 required building a high-throughput data pipeline. Tokenisation alone (converting text to integer token IDs) at 15T tokens takes approximately 2,000 CPU-hours on a modern machine at 2 GB/s throughput. The full pipeline (download, dedup, filter, tokenise, shuffle) typically requires a distributed computing cluster with hundreds of cores running for days to weeks.

# Estimate data pipeline throughput requirements
import math

TARGETS = {
    "Small SLM (1B, 20T tokens)": {"params": 1e9, "tokens": 20e9},
    "Medium (7B, Chinchilla)": {"params": 7e9, "tokens": 140e9},
    "Large (70B, Chinchilla)": {"params": 70e9, "tokens": 1_400e9},
    "LLaMA 3 8B (over-trained)": {"params": 8e9, "tokens": 15_000e9},
}

# Typical processing speeds
BYTES_PER_TOKEN = 4              # after tokenisation (int32)
RAW_BYTES_PER_TOKEN = 6          # raw text (before tokenisation)
CPU_THROUGHPUT_BYTES_PER_SEC = 500e6   # text processing: ~500 MB/s per core
DISK_THROUGHPUT_BYTES_PER_SEC = 500e6  # NVMe SSD

print(f"{'Model':<35} {'Tokens':>10} {'Raw data':>10} {'CPU-hours':>12} {'Storage (tokenised)':>20}")
print("-" * 92)
for name, cfg in TARGETS.items():
    tokens = cfg["tokens"]
    raw_gb = tokens * RAW_BYTES_PER_TOKEN / 1e9
    cpu_hours = tokens * RAW_BYTES_PER_TOKEN / CPU_THROUGHPUT_BYTES_PER_SEC / 3600
    storage_gb = tokens * BYTES_PER_TOKEN / 1e9

    print(
        f"{name:<35} {tokens/1e9:>8.0f}B {raw_gb:>8.0f}GB {cpu_hours:>10.0f}h {storage_gb:>16.0f}GB"
    )

print()
print("Note: 'CPU-hours' for tokenisation only; dedup/filter adds 5-10x more compute.")
print("At 100 CPU cores, 15T tokens = ~6,000 hours / 100 cores = ~60 hours wall time.")
Tooling and Ecosystem for Data Scaling

Pipeline framework for large-scale text processing. Handles CommonCrawl downloads, language identification, quality filtering, and deduplication. Used to build FineWeb, one of the best open pretraining datasets.
Python library for MinHash LSH (Locality Sensitive Hashing). Standard tool for fuzzy deduplication of web-scale text corpora.
OpenAI's fast BPE tokeniser. Essential for measuring token counts and building training datasets compatible with GPT-series models.
Provides access to The Pile, Common Crawl, FineWeb, RedPajama, and other curated pretraining datasets with Apache Arrow streaming for memory-efficient processing.
Allen AI's open-source toolkit for building LLM pretraining datasets. Includes classifiers for quality filtering and toxicity removal.
Security and Reliability in Data Scaling

Large pretraining datasets introduce several security and reliability risks that must be managed proactively. Data poisoning — injecting malicious content into the training corpus — is a realistic threat at web-crawl scale: a bad actor who controls a high-PageRank domain can inject content that influences model behaviour. Defences include URL allowlisting, content classifiers that detect adversarial patterns, and holdout red-teaming sets that probe for injected behaviours.

Benchmark contamination is a reliability risk: if training data contains test set examples from standard benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K), reported benchmark scores are inflated and do not reflect true generalisation. Standard practice is to remove 13-gram matches between training data and all known benchmarks before finalising the dataset.
Training data is a co-equal scaling axis
alongside model parameters. Under-training large models on too little data wastes compute.
— for every parameter, train on approximately 20 tokens. This maximises performance for a given compute budget.
over-trains small models by 10–100× Chinchilla-optimal, producing models that are cheap to serve while remaining competitive in quality.
A deduplicated, quality-filtered 500B token dataset consistently outperforms a raw 2T token dataset from the same source.
datatrove, datasketch, tiktoken, and the Hugging Face datasets library form the standard stack for building pretraining corpora.
from small pilot runs lets you predict full-scale performance before committing compute, enabling go/no-go decisions early.
are real reliability risks that require explicit defences in any serious pretraining pipeline.
